## T-Test

The T-test (Student's T-test) is a statistical hypothesis test, where the test statistic follows a Student's T-distribution. It is most commonly used to test if there is a statistically significant difference between the means of two populations. The T-test is similar to the Z-test, except the critical value comes from a T-distribution with certain degrees of freedom based on the sample size.

### One Sample T-Test

$H_0: \mu = \mu_0$

$H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0$

critical value: $t_{n-1,\alpha/2}$

T-score: $T=\frac{\bar{X}-\mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}}$

$H_0: \left\| T \right\| < t$

$H_1: \left\| T \right\| \geq t$

### Two Sample T-Test

1. Dependent samples:

    $d=X_1-X_2$

    $T=\frac{\bar{d}-\mu_0}{s_d/\sqrt{n}}$

2. Independent samples (population variances are assumed to be equal):

    $T=\frac{\bar{X}_1-\bar{X}_2}{\sqrt{s^2_p/n_1 + s^2_p/n_2}}$

    $s^2_{pool}=\frac{(n_1-1)s^2_1 + (n_2-1)s^2_2}{n_1+n_2-2}$

### Example

Test if the mean of the the first population is greater than the mean of the second.

$X_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu=12, \sigma^2=25)$

$X_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu=10, \sigma^2=25)$

$n_1=12$

$n_2=10$

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import ttest_ind

x_1 = np.random.normal(12, 5, 12)
x_2 = np.random.normal(10, 5, 10)

ttest_ind(x_1, x_2, alternative="greater")
```

```
TtestResult(statistic=np.float64(2.26296060071209), pvalue=np.float64(0.017460189327373483), df=np.float64(20.0))
```

```python
import pandas as pd
from scipy.stats import t

df = pd.DataFrame(
    data=[],
    columns=["critical value", "df", "t-score",
             "p-value", "alpha", "reject null"]
)

def t_test(alpha):
    ttest_result = ttest_ind(x_1, x_2, alternative="greater")
    critical_t = t.ppf(1-alpha, df=len(x_1) + len(x_2) - 2)
    df.loc[f"{alpha*100}%"] = [critical_t,
                               ttest_result.df,
                               ttest_result.statistic,
                               ttest_result.pvalue,
                               alpha,
                               alpha >= ttest_result.pvalue]

t_test(0.05)
t_test(0.01)
df
```

<div>
<style scoped>
    .dataframe tbody tr th:only-of-type {
        vertical-align: middle;
    }

    .dataframe tbody tr th {
        vertical-align: top;
    }

    .dataframe thead th {
        text-align: right;
    }
</style>
<table border="1" class="dataframe">
  <thead>
    <tr style="text-align: right;">
      <th></th>
      <th>critical value</th>
      <th>df</th>
      <th>t-score</th>
      <th>p-value</th>
      <th>alpha</th>
      <th>reject null</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <th>5.0%</th>
      <td>1.724718</td>
      <td>20.0</td>
      <td>2.262961</td>
      <td>0.01746</td>
      <td>0.05</td>
      <td>True</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <th>1.0%</th>
      <td>2.527977</td>
      <td>20.0</td>
      <td>2.262961</td>
      <td>0.01746</td>
      <td>0.01</td>
      <td>False</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
</div>

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(-5, 5, 100)
y = t.pdf(x, df=len(x_1) + len(x_2) - 2)

ttest = df.iloc[0]
alpha = np.array([(x, y) for x, y in zip(x, y) if x >= ttest["critical value"]])

plt.plot(x, y)
plt.fill_between(alpha[:,0], alpha[:,1], color="red", alpha=0.5, label="alpha=0.05")

x_t = np.linspace(ttest["t-score"]-5, ttest["t-score"]+5, 100)
y_t = t.pdf(x, df=len(x_1) + len(x_2) - 2)
plt.axvline(ttest["t-score"], c="red", linestyle="dashed", label="T-Score")
plt.plot(x_t, y_t, c="lightblue")

p = np.array([(x, y) for x, y in zip(x, y) if x >= ttest["t-score"]])
plt.fill_between(p[:,0], p[:,1], color="lightblue", alpha=0.8, label="p-value")

alpha_1 = np.array([(x, y) for x, y in zip(x, y) if x >= df.iloc[1]["critical value"]])
plt.fill_between(alpha_1[:,0], alpha_1[:,1], color="orange", alpha=0.5, label="alpha=0.01")

plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

```
<Figure size 640x480 with 1 Axes>
```

<img alt="No description has been provided for this image" class="" src="data:image/png;base64,"/>

### Welch's T-Test

In contrast to Student's T-test, Welch's T-test does not assume the population's variances to be equal. There are two differences in the calculation, compared to Student's T-test:

Test statistic:

$T=\frac{\bar{X}_1-\bar{X}_2}{\sqrt{s^2_1/n_1+s^2_2/n_2}}$

Calculating the test statistic does not involve pooling the variances.

Degrees of freedom:

$\nu=\Large\frac{s^2_1/n_1+s^2_2/n_2}{\frac{(s^2_1/n_1)^2}{n_1-1}+\frac{(s^2_2/n_2)^2}{n_2-1}}$

The formula for the degrees of freedom takes the difference in variances into account. If the variances are equal, the degrees of freedom of Welch's T-test will be the same as in Student's T-test.

#### Student's T-Test

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import ttest_ind

x_1 = np.random.normal(loc=10, scale=8, size=23)
x_2 = np.random.normal(loc=15, scale=10, size=26)

ttest_ind(x_1, x_2, alternative="less")
```

```
TtestResult(statistic=np.float64(-2.607608518057224), pvalue=np.float64(0.006091125839537305), df=np.float64(47.0))
```

#### Welch's T-Test

```python
ttest_ind(x_1, x_2, alternative="less", equal_var=False)
```

```
TtestResult(statistic=np.float64(-2.7029625465958573), pvalue=np.float64(0.0050848653392887196), df=np.float64(38.52016278973386))
```

#### When to use Welch's T-test

You could always use Welch's instead of Student's T-test when comparing independent groups. Welch's T-test is as accurate as Student's T-test if population variances are the same, but it gives more accurate results if the variances are different. In practice, it is unlikely anyway that independent groups have the same variances.
